title: What kind of data do you feed your LLM to make trade decisions?
author: Sweet_Brief6914
selftext:
I'm in the middle of developing my first AI Trading bot, and I'm curious what kind of data you feed your bot? 

So far, I've designed 2 main strategies, mean reversion and trend following, and I'd feed RSI, MACD, CCI, MFI, and ATR values of the last 14 1h candles and closing prices of the last 30 days, with clear-cut criteria on when to enter based on the indicators and the news, what else do you guys have your LLM looking into before making decisions on the trades?

comments.author: SeagullMan2
comments.body: This is a great example of how not to use an LLM

replies.author: Sweet_Brief6914
replies.body: What would be a great example of how to use an LLM then? :D

replies.author: SeagullMan2
replies.body: You can use an LLM to explain why LLM architecture is not designed to handle quantitative trading data or make trading decisions. Then you can use the LLM to generate python code for an actual trading bot.

replies.author: Sweet_Brief6914
replies.body:
>Then you can use the LLM to generate python code for an actual trading bot.

Appreciate the advice, good news is that it's already been established! [https://imgur.com/a/G05gJjT](https://imgur.com/a/G05gJjT) these bots went live in August, but I'm really curious how I could push AI to make trading decisions for me based on indicators data and OHLC ones + news sentiment and some strategies outlined for it, I've already designed a prompt that reflects how I'd be trading if I did it manually (the beginning of a disaster, I know), and I wanna see what other guys have come up with, not asking for the strategies themselves, but more keen to learn the methodology

I am looking to sell it to a serious person because I dont have enough money to trade it for living.

Anyone interested can DM me I will provide you the report

It is Bitcoin....

As it's ML architecture I feed it with recent price data and re-train the model over the span of x days.... And in that way it get characteristics of new market data and thus it adapts.

replies.author: Important_Worth_8382
replies.body:
It looks good. But it’s just a backtest. Have you looked at prop trading?  
Have you thought about hooking it up to a paper account and comparing the results? It will also give you a better test this way.

replies.author: Spirited_Syllabub488
replies.body: Yeah, I have been trading it for 7 months now and the results are similar. To be honest, better than backtest stats. Got 140% net ROI in 7 months, no losing month.

replies.author: mespejo100
replies.body: Has probado con cuentas de fondeo como FTMO.com? debes hacer un challenge y te prestan de 10k a 400k para que operes... maxima perdida diaria 5%, si no tienes dinero debes dirigirte a las cuentas de fondeo directamente...

replies.author: Spirited_Syllabub488
replies.body: Yes I am aware of that but my strategy is not designed for prop firms. In order to match the thresholds of prop firms I have to reduce my required risk and that will also reduce my returns and it would take me months to pass the challenges.

comments.author: AwesomeThyme777
comments.body: When people mention AI training bots, they're usually referring to ML/DL models trained on exclusively that financial data, not transformers trained on language. LLMs, regardless of what data you feed them, are fundamentally destined to fail at profitably trading, since that's simply not what they were trained for. This website runs exactly that experiment [https://nof1.ai/](https://nof1.ai/) and as you can see, all of the different models are currently losing money. I highly advise against this.

replies.author: Sweet_Brief6914
replies.body:
tjhe guys at nof1, do they provide what kind of prompt theyve engineered to the models and what kind of data theyre giving them? how often? what strats theyre following? 

I'm gonna say that without knowing these variables, it's hardly an experiment, we can't come at a definitive conclusion that LLMs dont work, because one could always argue that problably they messed up the prompt and data part? 

I'm gonna run the experiment with a method I think could work, I didn't do any backtests, kinda lazy to develop an entire envrionment for it right now, but I'll put it on a prop firm demo account and it'll compete against my bots, I'll get back to this sub once the account is breached or once it actually generates 10% of profits, my criteria for failing would be if the ai trades compound into 10% loss.

replies.author: AwesomeThyme777
replies.body: That is a good point about the prompting and I was wondering the same my self. They do give a brief overview or the general idea of the prompt, but not the whole thing. I look forward to seeing your results too

replies.author: Sweet_Brief6914
replies.body: Where do they give the general idea? I've looked at their website and didn't find anything

replies.author: AwesomeThyme777
replies.body: At the top it says new baseline, monk mode, etc. if you click on these you can see what the gist of the prompt is

replies.author: Alert_Attention_5905
replies.body:
You know you can build a trading system that has a classic mechanical live trader plus a trained AI that monitors the system, the mechanical trader, and the economy. Still technically an AI trader even though AI reasoning isn't built into trade signals.

You can even train the AI on strategy design so it designs new strategies for the mechanical trader, who backtests them and filters them with thresholds.

AI traders are absolutely 100% profitable if you build them right.

replies.author: AwesomeThyme777
replies.body: is that possible to manage without human intervention given the current state of AI we have right now?

replies.author: Alert_Attention_5905
replies.body: Yes it's 100% possible.

replies.author: gumifan1991
replies.body: nof1 is a brand new company throwing LLMs at these problems with limited context / infrastructure to support them. not really indicative of LLMs' trading capabilities since it's effectively random and they're trading with instruments that are extremely punishing (makes no sense to trade with leverage when you have no edge).

replies.author: Patient-Bumblebee
replies.body:
They cant come up with strategies, but they can definitely automate existing ones. Thats how we should be using LLM's. Not to get rich but to automate stuff and free up time.

Look at [Everstrike](https://everstrike.io) for an example of what I mean.

comments.author: Mindless_Routine_721
comments.body:
I built an AI agent (using OpenAI API) specialized for the Korean stock market. Unlike typical bots, I tried to make it judge intuitively like a human would.

Once candidate stocks are identified, the system writes a detailed PDF report for each stock. The trading agent then uses this report as feed to make buy/sell decisions.

The PDF report includes roughly these data points.

1. Technical analysis: 2 years of OHLCV, volume, market cap trends (modern LLMs are good enough to infer basic indicators from this data - they nail support/resistance levels perfectly)

2. Investor transaction analysis: 2 years of trading volume breakdown by foreign/retail/institutional investors

3. Fundamental analysis: 5 years of financial data + disclosure data

4. News analysis & sector leader analysis: Recent news for the stock + trend analysis of sector leaders

5. Market & macroeconomic analysis

I format all this data like an analyst report, feed it to the LLM, and ask it to write trading scenarios. Probabilistically, it makes pretty decent investment decisions.

I've open-sourced my strategy if you want to check it out. Core agents and prompts are under the cores/agents directory.

https://github.com/dragon1086/prism-insight

comments.author: gumifan1991
comments.body:
I mostly trade crypto so I'm looking at OI perps data and filtering as much noise as I can (finding directional flow vs. delta neutral). It's kind of hard to do because delta neutral traders usually have different legs on different venues but combine that with some classic indicators and you can generally figure out where the market is moving.

I mostly use Cod3x for all of this (full disclosure i also own their token but the platform is actually good).

comments.author: Ok_Tooth7545
comments.body:
I'm currently building this.

a few observations over the past days:

1) LLM's are trained on a vast amount of past data, this is often why you would see "as of October 2023 the price of {ticker} is...", this concludes that with no actual context to some recent time frame and real data it's not going to work.

2) LLM's are amazing language models but lack basic "quantities skills" that's why o3 take 2 minutes to figure out "how many r's are there in the word strawberry"... my second conclusion is the ll  would need hard coded qauntative function tools.

3) market data... this one I'm still exploring, but in a nutshell as a human I would probably at the market open my laptop, look at a heat map, see ticker_x up 7% before the open, open a new tab with that tickers graph, explore the dialy , weekly and monthly candel, scan news ect.... I feel like just using a vision model to feed this to a llm is bound to fail... the amount of details and features we humans can comprehend I think in the vision area is still greater than llms.... my current thought are trying to find features that matter (PCA) and maybe feed that to the LLM, or try find some 80/20 rule pro traders use ... 

4) stale data... weather that's a stocks news, current price, current volume ... as soon as data is fetched its stale depending on what candle its trying to trade... as a human we watch thr graph update and pay "less attention" to older candels as new data flows in ... RAG , Caching or anything like that I feel are out of the question for a llm agent trader


would love to here other Insights
